items.foco: Classification of dengue according to WHO
items.title: WHO Classification 2009
items.contentHtml:
Classification of dengue according to WHO

  • The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies dengue into dengue without warning signs, dengue with warning signs and severe dengue (2009 update).
  • Dengue without warning signs includes fever plus two criteria: headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, leukopenia or mild hemorrhagic manifestations.
  • Dengue with warning signs requires at least one warning sign (see focus 2).
  • Severe dengue is defined by severe plasma extravasation, severe hemorrhage or significant organic damage.
  • This classification replaces the previous one of classic dengue and hemorrhagic dengue, being more sensitive to detect cases that require hospitalization.

items.title: Warning signs
items.contentHtml:
Alarm signs in dengue

  • The alarm signs according to the World Health Organization (WHO) are: intense and continuous abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, accumulation of fluids (ascites, pleural effusion), mucosal bleeding, lethargy or irritability, hepatomegaly >2 cm and increased hematocrit concurrent with a rapid drop in platelets.
  • The presence of a single warning sign indicates risk of progression to severe dengue and requires hospitalization and strict monitoring.
  • Painful hepatomegaly is a particularly relevant sign in children.
  • The combination of elevated hematocrit and thrombocytopenia is an early marker of extravasation. plasma.

items.title: tourniquet test
items.contentHtml:
Positive tourniquet test

  • The tourniquet test is performed by inflating the blood pressure cuff to a midpoint between the systolic and diastolic pressure for 5 minutes (in children 3 minutes).
  • It is considered positive if 20 or more petechiae appear in an area of 2.5 cm x 2.5 cm (approximately the size of a square of the sphygmomanometer) on the anterior surface of the forearm.
  • This test evaluates capillary fragility and thrombocytopenia; It has a variable (moderate) sensitivity and is more useful in the early febrile phase.
  • It can be negative in severe dengue due to shock, which does not rule out the disease.
  • It is not specific, since it can be positive in leptospirosis, rickettsiosis or other febrile diseases.

items.title: Severe dengue criteria
items.contentHtml:
Severe dengue: diagnostic criteria

  • Severe dengue is defined by at least one of the following: severe plasma extravasation leading to shock (dengue shock syndrome) or respiratory distress due to massive spills; severe hemorrhage (according to clinical criteria, e.g. hematemesis, melena, central nervous system bleeding); or severe organ damage such as hepatitis (aspartate aminotransferase or alanine aminotransferase >1000 international units per liter), myocarditis, pancreatitis, encephalitis, or acute renal failure.
  • Dengue shock syndrome is characterized by weak pulse, tachycardia, cold extremities, capillary refill >2 seconds, and pulse pressure <20 millimeters mercury.
  • The presence of platelets <100,000 per microliter is common but does not alone define severe dengue.

items.title: Dengue vs leptospirosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with leptospira

  • Both present with fever, myalgia and headache; but leptospirosis (caused by Leptospira interrogans) usually presents jaundice (especially in Weil's disease), conjunctival injection and calf pain, less frequent findings in dengue.
  • The tourniquet test can be positive in both, so it is not useful for differentiate.
  • In leptospirosis, creatine phosphokinase is usually elevated due to myositis, and kidney function is affected early with oliguria or proteinuria.
  • The confirmatory diagnosis in leptospirosis is microagglutination (Martin-Pettit test) or culture; in dengue the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) or specific serology.
  • Treatment of leptospirosis requires doxycycline or penicillin, while dengue is supportive.

items.foco: Management of febrile phase and fluid replacement
items.title: Water management febrile phase
items.contentHtml:
Management of febrile phase and fluid replacement

  • In the febrile phase (days 1-3) the objective is to maintain oral hydration with oral rehydration solution (oral serum) at a rate of 5-10 milliliters per kilogram per hour according to tolerance and losses.
  • If there is vomiting or moderate dehydration, lactate is indicated Intravenous Ringer's (not 0.9% saline alone, due to lower potassium and buffer content).
  • Intravenous replacement in the critical phase (around day 4-6) should be cautious: only isotonic crystalloids (Ringer's lactate) and adjust according to hematocrit and vital signs.
  • Avoid water overload: in dengue with warning signs, administer 5-7 milliliters per kilogram per hour in 1-2 hours, then reduce to 3-5 milliliters per kilogram per hour if there is improvement.
  • Do not use routine colloids; reserve for refractory shock.

items.title: Hospitalization in dengue
items.contentHtml:
Indications for hospitalization in dengue

  • Hospitalization is indicated in the presence of any warning sign (see focus 2).
  • Also if there are decompensated comorbidities such as diabetes mellitus, high blood pressure, chronic kidney disease, pregnancy, obesity or extreme age (infants and the elderly).
  • If the patient presents hemorrhage active (e.g. uncontrollable epistaxis, gingival bleeding, hematemesis) or severe thrombocytopenia (<50,000 platelets per microliter) with risk of bleeding.
  • The lack of oral tolerance (persistent vomiting) or moderate to severe dehydration that is not corrected orally.
  • The increased hematocrit >20% of baseline value suggests plasma extravasation and requires in-hospital monitoring.

items.title: Paracetamol and avoiding NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
Use of paracetamol, avoid non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)

  • The drug of choice for fever and pain in dengue is paracetamol (acetaminophen) at doses of 10-15 milligrams per kilogram per dose every 6 hours (maximum 60 milligrams per kilogram per day in children, 4 grams per day in adults).
  • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac, aspirin are contraindicated due to the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and Reye's syndrome (especially aspirin in children).
  • NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation and can exacerbate thrombocytopenia and bleeding tendency. of dengue.
  • Do not use corticosteroids routinely, as they have not shown benefit and may increase the risk of secondary infections.

items.foco: Platelet transfusion: when to indicate it
items.title: platelet transfusion
items.contentHtml:
Platelet transfusion: when to indicate it

  • Platelet transfusion in dengue is not indicated prophylactically only due to a low count; Most bleeding in dengue is due to vasculopathy and not only due to thrombocytopenia.
  • It is indicated when there is active hemorrhage (e.g. bleeding from the central nervous system, massive gastrointestinal, hemoptysis) associated with severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000 platelets per microliter) or <50,000 if there is major bleeding.
  • In emergency surgery or invasive procedures, maintain platelets >50,000 per microliter.
  • The usual dose is 1 unit of apheresis or 1 unit of single donor platelets per 10 kilograms of weight.
  • Do not use in dengue shock without bleeding, as transfusion may worsen fluid overload.

items.foco: IgM and IgG serology in dengue
items.title: IgM and IgG serology
items.contentHtml:
IgM and IgG serology in dengue

  • The immunoglobulin M (IgM) becomes detectable from day 3-5 of fever, reaches a peak at 7-10 days and persists for 2-3 months; a positive result indicates recent or current infection.
  • Immunoglobulin G (IgG) appears around day 7-10 and persists for years; High titers in the acute phase suggest secondary infection (greater risk of severe dengue).
  • In primary infection, IgM predominates and IgG appears late; in secondary infection, IgG rises rapidly (anamnestic) and IgM may be low or absent.
  • The IgM test by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is the most used; polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is useful in the first 5 days to detect viral ribonucleic acid.
  • False IgM positivity can occur due to cross-reactivity with other flaviviruses (such as Zika or yellow fever).

items.title: CYD-TDV Vaccine (Dengvaxia)
items.contentHtml:
Prevention with CYD-TDV vaccine

  • The CYD-TDV vaccine (Dengvaxia) is a live attenuated quadrivalent vaccine based on the yellow fever virus skeleton.
  • It is indicated only in people aged 9 to 45 years with confirmed previous dengue infection (seropositive), since the risk increases in seronegative people of severe dengue by antibody-dependent enhancement.
  • It is administered in 3 doses (0, 6 and 12 months).
  • In seropositives it reduces the risk of severe dengue and hospitalization by approximately 80%.
  • It is not recommended in areas where seroprevalence is low (<50%) without screening Previous.
  • Other vaccines (TAK-003) are under study; The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends individual evaluation of serological status before vaccination.

items.foco: Sequence of infection and risk of severe dengue
items.title: Sequential infection and risk
items.contentHtml:
Sequence of infection and risk of severe dengue

  • Severe dengue is associated with secondary infection by a different serotype of dengue virus (DENV-1, 2, 3 or 4), due to the phenomenon of antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE).
  • In the first infection (primary), the antibodies generated are neutralizing against the infecting serotype, but do not protect against other serotypes.
  • In the second infection, pre-existing non-neutralizing antibodies facilitate the entry of the virus into Fc-receptor positive cells (monocytes/macrophages), increasing viral replication and the inflammatory response.
  • This deregulated response produces cytokine storm, plasma extravasation and increased risk of shock and hemorrhage.
  • The increased risk of Severe dengue occurs with sequential infection with DENV-2 after DENV-1, or DENV-3 after DENV-2.
